title: Pseudoentanglement in constant depth: How trivial states can have non-trivial entanglement structure
abstract: We construct a family of 2D-local constant-depth quantum circuits that output states whose entanglement entropy across a specified cut cannot be estimated in quantum polynomial time. As constant-depth quantum circuits can be learned from polynomially many quantum samples, our resulting pseudoentangled states are implicitly public-key and not pseudorandom. This separates pseudoentanglement from pseudorandomness in the shallow-circuit regime: the former is possible, while the latter is not. The construction is based on the quantum intractability of the Dense-Sparse Learning Parity with Noise problem introduced in [DJ25] and uses a bounded-fan-in, bounded-fan-out classical randomized encoding for linear maps $\mathbf{x} \mapsto \mathbf{Mx},$ which could be of independent interest. As applications, we obtain quantum hardness for the problem of learning the entanglement structure (across a fixed cut) of the ground-state of 1D and 2D local Hamiltonians. The 1D Hamiltonian has an inverse polynomial gap, whereas the 2D one has a constant gap. This complements the result of [BZZ24] that showed only factoring-based hardness for the 1D case, though achieving a volume versus area entanglement difference.
summary: This paper constructs 'pseudoentangled' quantum states using shallow, local circuits whose entanglement structure is hard for quantum computers to measure. By mapping these states to local Hamiltonians, the authors prove that determining the ground state entanglement structure of 1D and 2D quantum systems is quantumly hard, provided certain cryptographic assumptions hold.
main_problem: The paper aims to construct quantum states whose entanglement structure is computationally hard to determine (pseudoentanglement), thereby separating this property from pseudorandomness in the shallow-circuit regime.
main_result: It constructs families of 2D-local, constant-depth quantum circuits whose output states exhibit a large, non-negligible gap in entanglement entropy across a fixed cut, demonstrating quantum hardness for learning the entanglement structure of local Hamiltonians.
method: The construction relies on the quantum intractability of the Dense-Sparse Learning Parity with Noise problem, utilizing a perfect randomized encoding of a linear map to generate the pseudoentangled states.
model_or_system: The model involves 2D-local, constant-depth quantum circuits acting on qubits, which are then mapped to local Hamiltonians on 1D and 2D lattices for physical applications.
key_observables: Entanglement Entropy (specifically the gap $\Delta(n)$ across a fixed cut $W$), and the spectral gap of the resulting Hamiltonians.
important_parameters: Circuit Depth (constant depth), System Size ($n$), and the gap size ($\Delta(n) = \omega(\log n)$).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is the intractability of the Dense-Sparse LPN problem for quantum polynomial-time (QPT) adversaries.
figures_summary: Figures schematically illustrate the 2D layout of the randomized encoding and the local implementation of a single plaquette, detailing how the output bits depend on input masks and neighboring bits.
paper_structure: The paper builds from cryptographic preliminaries (randomized encoding) to the main pseudoentanglement theorem, and finally applies this result to prove quantum hardness for learning the entanglement structure of ground states of 1D and 2D local Hamiltonians.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful tool to establish computational hardness for characterizing the ground state properties of quantum many-body systems, which is crucial for understanding the limits of quantum simulation and quantum phase detection.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Schematic 2D layout of the randomized encoding with four input columns and three representative rows. Sky-blue boxes are input bits, vermillion boxes are mask bits, and purple boxes are outputs. The w-outputs are computed by column-local nearest-neighbor gadgets: wj,0 depends only on xj and rj,1, while wj,1 and wj,2 depend only on adjacent r-masks in the same column. The z-outputs are computed inside constant-size plaquettes: zi,j depends only on the two neighboring horizontal masks si,j−1 and si,j, and, when Mi,j = 1, the local vertical mask rj,i. The vertical wires through the w-boxes should be read as connections to the adjacent r-masks above and below them, not as connections to...

Fig. 2: Local implementation of one plaquette. The four external ports carry rj,i from above, rj,i+1 to the row below, and the two horizontal masks si,j−1 and si,j. The plaquette computes wj,i = rj,i ⊕rj,i+1 and zi,j = si,j−1 ⊕si,j ⊕Mi,jrj,i. The gray register aj,i is only local workspace, initialized to 0 and uncomputed to 0 after use. One possible local schedule is as follows. First copy rj,i into the workspace, apply it to wj,i, and uncompute the workspace: rj,i →aj,i →wj,i followed by rj,i →aj,i. Then apply the incoming rj,i+1 from the bottom port to wj,i. Next reuse the same workspace for the horizontal mask: copy si,j−1 into aj,i, apply it to zi,j, and uncompute it; then apply si,j...
